[PASS] Phase reversal code: Correctly implements electrode voltages with distance-dependent falloff, then computes bipolar derivations by subtraction – mathematically correct.
[PASS] End-of-chain limitation: correctly noted that sources at Fp1 or O1 lack full phase reversal.

60 Hz: perfect sinusoidal, all channels equally – correct

[PASS] Eye blink amplitude ~100-200 uV – correct.

[MINOR] The eye blink artifact is described as a “positive deflection” at Fp1/Fp2. This is correct by standard EEG convention (negative up), but could be confusing for first-year medical students who may not know the EEG polarity convention. The tutorial does not explicitly state the convention (negative up vs. positive up) anywhere. A one-sentence note clarifying this would help. However, the blink is correctly described as being “positive” at the frontopolar electrodes, which matches the corneoretinal dipole physics.

[PASS] Hanning window applied to each chunk: 0.5 * (1 - cos(2*pi*i/(N-1))) – correct Hann window formula.
[PASS] Time-frequency tradeoff: smaller window = better time resolution, worse frequency resolution; and vice versa – correct.

Standard Holter monitors typically sample at 125-250 Hz (for rhythm monitoring, HRV analysis). The AHA-recommended diagnostic ECG bandwidth is 0.05-150 Hz, but this refers to diagnostic 12-lead ECG, not Holter.
500 Hz is typical of high-fidelity diagnostic ECG or stress-test systems, not standard ambulatory Holter monitors.

The listing mixes Holter-specific context in the label with diagnostic-ECG numbers in the frequency range and sampling rate.
Recommendation: Either relabel as “ECG (diagnostic)” with frequency range 0.05-150 Hz and typical rate 250-500 Hz, OR keep the Holter label but adjust to “0.5-40 Hz” (typical Holter monitoring bandwidth) with typical rate “125-250 Hz.” Alternatively, split into two rows (Holter vs. diagnostic).
Why critical: A student or instructor might look this up and find standard Holter monitors run at 125-200 Hz, contradicting the table. This could undermine trust in the rest of the tutorial’s clinical accuracy.

title: Tutorial Verification Report
text:
Nyquist attribution (Module 2): “Harry Nyquist figured this out in the 1920s” – Nyquist’s 1928 paper addressed signaling rate, not the sampling theorem per se. The sampling theorem was formally stated by Shannon in 1949. Suggestion: “The insight traces back to work by Harry Nyquist in the 1920s and was formalized by Claude Shannon in 1949.”
Ideal filter visualization (Module 3): The bandpass filter demo uses brick-wall ideal filtering (frequencies are either fully passed or fully blocked). Real filters have transition bands. The quiz feedback for Q2 mentions this, but a brief note in the main instructional text would be more visible.
T3/T4/T5/T6 nomenclature (Module 5): The tutorial uses the classic 10-20 names. The ACNS recommended renaming T3->T7, T4->T8, T5->P7, T6->P8 in the modified 10-10 system (2006). Both are in clinical use. Consider adding a parenthetical: “(T3, also called T7 in the 10-10 system)” on first use.
Theta amplitude (Module 5): Listed as 50-100 uV. This is at the high end. Many references give 20-100 uV. Not wrong, but the lower bound could be adjusted to 20-30 uV to be more inclusive.
Eye blink polarity convention (Module 6): The blink is correctly described as “positive” at Fp1/Fp2, but the tutorial never states EEG polarity convention. First-year students may not know whether “positive” means the trace goes up or down on screen. A one-sentence note would clarify.
Quiz radio button name collision (Module 4 vs Module 3): Both modules use name="fq1", name="fq2", name="fq3" for their quiz radio buttons. If both modules were ever rendered on the same page (unlikely in Quarto’s per-page rendering, but possible in a single-page build), the radio buttons would conflict.

title: Tutorial Verification Report
text:
Add EEG polarity convention note. Somewhere in Module 1 or Module 5, mention that clinical EEG displays typically use “negative up” convention, which affects how waveforms appear on screen. This will prevent confusion when students see real EEG traces.
Module 2 linear interpolation note. The sampling widget uses linear interpolation for reconstruction. Real DAC reconstruction uses sinc interpolation (ideal) or zero-order hold. A brief note like “This demo connects the dots with straight lines for simplicity. Real systems use more sophisticated reconstruction, but the aliasing problem is the same” would be instructionally honest.
Module 5 frequency band boundary note. The boundaries between EEG bands (e.g., alpha at 8-13 Hz vs. 8-12 Hz) vary across sources. A brief note acknowledging this – “These boundaries are approximate; some textbooks define alpha as 8-12 Hz” – would prevent students from being confused when they encounter different numbers in other resources.
Module 7 seizure evolution. The “Seizure-like Preset” goes Alpha (10 Hz) -> Delta (2 Hz) -> Beta (20 Hz). A more realistic seizure evolution pattern would be: normal baseline -> low-frequency rhythmic onset -> progressive increase in frequency (the “frequency evolution” the quiz describes) -> abrupt termination -> post-ictal suppression. The current preset (Alpha -> Delta -> Beta) is somewhat inverted from the classic pattern. Consider relabeling it or adjusting the frequencies to better match the clinical vignette in the module’s intro.

title: Tutorial Verification Report
text:
The module ordering is logical and well-scaffolded:

Signals – establishes vocabulary (amplitude, frequency, phase)
Sampling – explains how continuous signals become digital
Filtering – teaches noise removal
FFT – reveals frequency content
EEG Basics – applies all prior concepts to the clinical domain 5b. Montages & Dipoles – extends EEG understanding with display and localization

Each module explicitly references prior modules and builds on established concepts. The clinical vignettes are realistic and appropriate for first-year medical students. The interactive widgets reinforce concepts effectively.
No oversimplifications reach the level of being misleading. The tutorial is appropriately pitched for the audience – it makes justified simplifications (ideal filters, pure sine wave components, linear interpolation) while being honest about them where it matters most.

title: Module 6: Artifacts
text:
A medical student is shadowing in the epilepsy monitoring unit. The patient’s EEG suddenly shows a massive, sharp deflection. The student’s eyes go wide: “Is that a seizure?!” The attending neurologist glances at the screen, then at the patient, and says flatly: “No. She blinked.” Knowing the difference between an artifact and a seizure could change a diagnosis — or prevent an unnecessary one.
You can now read an EEG — electrode positions, frequency bands, basic patterns. But here’s the dirty secret: a lot of what shows up on the EEG screen isn’t brain activity at all. Eye blinks, muscle tension, heartbeats, loose electrodes, power lines — they all contaminate the recording. An experienced neurologist can spot these instantly. By the end of this module, so can you.

title: Module 6: Artifacts
text:
The Five Usual Suspects
Every EEG reader learns to recognize these five artifacts on sight:









Artifact
Source
Key Feature
Where It’s Worst




Eye blink
Corneoretinal dipole
Large, slow, positive deflection
Fp1, Fp2 (frontal)


Muscle (EMG)
Jaw clenching, frowning
High-frequency “fuzz”
Temporal electrodes


60 Hz line noise
Power lines
Perfect sinusoidal ripple
All channels equally


ECG
Heart’s electrical field
Periodic sharp waves at heart rate
Diffuse, ~1 Hz


Electrode pop
Loose or faulty electrode
Sudden spike, single channel
Just one electrode



Let’s learn to recognize each one — and then practice.

title: Module 6: Artifacts
text:
Interactive: Artifact Anatomy
Click each tab to see how the artifact looks in the time domain, what it looks like in the frequency domain, and how to deal with it clinically.



  
    Eye Blink
    Muscle
    60 Hz Noise
    ECG
    Electrode Pop
  
  
  
    
      Time Domain
      
    
    
      Frequency Spectrum
      
    
  






Explore each artifact type:

Click through all five tabs. For each one, compare the time domain (left) and frequency spectrum (right).
Notice how eye blinks add energy at low frequencies, while muscle artifact adds energy at high frequencies.
The 60 Hz line noise has the most distinctive frequency signature — a single sharp spike at exactly 60 Hz.
For the electrode pop, notice how only ONE channel would be affected (here shown as a single sharp transient).

title: Module 6: Artifacts
section: Interactive: “Brain or Noise?” — The Artifact Game
text:
Interactive: “Brain or Noise?” — The Artifact Game
Time to test yourself. Each trial shows a synthetic EEG segment. Is it real brain activity, or is it contaminated by an artifact? Click your answer, get instant feedback, and track your score.

  
    Trial 1 of 8
    Score: 0 / 0
  
  
  
    
      Brain Activity
    
    
      Artifact
    
  
  
  
    
      Next →
    
  




Tips for the game:

Look for sudden, large deflections that don’t match the ongoing background — could be a blink or electrode pop.
Listen for high-frequency fuzz — that’s muscle.
Look for perfect regularity — 60 Hz noise or ECG.
Clean EEG has a natural, somewhat irregular rhythm. Artifacts tend to stand out because they don’t “fit” the background.



Artifact rejection is not optional. If you analyze contaminated EEG, your results are wrong. Garbage in, garbage out. The three main strategies are:

Prevention — Good electrode application, low impedances, relaxed patient
Rejection — Identify and discard contaminated epochs
Correction — Use signal processing (notch filters, ICA) to remove the artifact while keeping the brain signal

In clinical practice, you’ll use all three. Prevention is always best.

title: Module 6: Artifacts
text:
Why Artifacts Fool People
Here’s the thing that makes artifacts dangerous: some artifacts look like real brain pathology.

An eye blink can look like a frontal spike (which could mean epilepsy)
A muscle artifact can look like high-frequency beta activity (which has clinical significance)
An ECG artifact can look like periodic discharges (which can indicate serious brain injury)

The difference is in the distribution (which channels are affected), the morphology (exact shape), and the context (what the patient was doing). A skilled EEG reader checks all three before making a call.

The artifact checklist — Before calling something abnormal, ask:

Is it in only one electrode? Probably electrode pop or bad contact.
Is it only in frontal channels? Could be an eye blink.
Is it perfectly periodic? Check if it matches the heart rate (ECG) or a power-line harmonic (60/120 Hz).
Is it broadband and “fuzzy”? Likely muscle.
Does it correlate with patient behavior? Ask the tech: “Was the patient doing anything when this happened?”

title: Module 6: Artifacts
text:
Quiz: Check Your Understanding

  Quiz

  
    Q1: A sudden large positive deflection appears simultaneously in Fp1 and Fp2 but not in occipital channels. This is most likely:
    
       A. A seizure — it's a big voltage change
    
    
       B. Eye blink artifact — frontally distributed, positive polarity
    
    
       C. 60 Hz line noise

    Q2: An EEG recording shows high-frequency "fuzz" predominantly over temporal electrodes. The patient was chewing gum. This is:

title: Module 6: Artifacts
text:
What’s Next
You can now tell brain from noise. With clean EEG in hand, there’s one more power tool to learn: instead of looking at frequencies averaged over the whole recording, what if you could watch them change in real time? That’s the spectrogram — and it’s how neurologists track brain dynamics as they unfold.

← Module 5b: Montages & Dipoles Module 7: Spectrograms →

title: Module 3: Filtering
text:
A neurology resident is reviewing an EEG recording. The tracing is a mess — every channel shows a fast, jittery oscillation riding on top of the brain waves. It’s 60 Hz power line noise, picked up from the hospital’s electrical wiring. The attending says: “Apply a notch filter at 60 Hz.” The resident clicks a button. The noise vanishes. The underlying alpha rhythm is now clearly visible. But what just happened? Did the filter remove only the noise? Could it have removed something important from the brain signal too?
Your EEG recording is full of stuff you don’t want. 60 Hz power line noise. Slow electrode drift (the baseline wanders up and down over seconds). Muscle artifact from the patient clenching their jaw. Filtering is how you separate what matters from what doesn’t — like adjusting the bass and treble on a stereo, but for brain waves.
The idea is simple: every signal is a mixture of different frequencies. A filter lets certain frequencies through and blocks others. The question is always: which frequencies do you keep?

title: Module 3: Filtering
text:
Filtering in Action
Watch the animation below. A synthetic “raw EEG” – built from slow drift, a 10 Hz alpha rhythm, and 60 Hz power line noise – gets decomposed in the frequency domain. A 1–40 Hz bandpass filter mask grays out the unwanted frequencies, and the time-domain signal transforms from noisy to clean.


  
    
    Your browser does not support the video tag.
  
  
    Bandpass filtering removes slow drift and 60 Hz noise, leaving only the 10 Hz alpha rhythm.

title: Module 3: Filtering
section: The Anatomy of a Dirty Signal
text:
The Anatomy of a Dirty Signal
Before we filter anything, let’s see what we’re dealing with. The signal below is synthetic, but it’s a realistic mix of what you’d see in a real EEG recording:

A slow drift (0.3 Hz) (baseline wander from electrode impedance changes)
A 10 Hz alpha rhythm (the brain signal you actually want)
A 60 Hz line noise (power line interference)
Some random noise (thermal noise from electronics)




  
    Choose a filter:
    
      Raw Signal
    
    
      High-pass (1 Hz)
    
    
      Low-pass (40 Hz)
    
    
      Band-pass (1–40 Hz)
    
  
  
    Showing: Raw signal — all components present.
  
  
  




Experiment:

Click Raw — this is what the technician sees before any filtering. Hard to read, right?
Click High-pass (1 Hz) — the slow drift disappears, but the 60 Hz fuzz is still there.
Click Low-pass (40 Hz) — the 60 Hz noise vanishes, but now the slow drift is back.
Click Band-pass (1–40 Hz) — both the drift and line noise are gone. This is the standard clinical EEG filter. The 10 Hz alpha wave is clearly visible.



The three basic filter types:

High-pass filter: Removes frequencies below the cutoff. Lets high frequencies through. Removes slow drift.
Low-pass filter: Removes frequencies above the cutoff. Lets low frequencies through. Removes fast noise.
Band-pass filter: Combination of high-pass and low-pass. Only lets frequencies between two cutoffs through. This is what you’ll use most in clinical EEG.

The standard clinical EEG display filter is 1–40 Hz band-pass (sometimes 0.5–70 Hz for more detail).

title: Module 3: Filtering
text:
Interactive: Adjustable Band-Pass Filter
Now you control the filter cutoffs. Drag the sliders and watch the signal change in real time. The small plot on the right shows the filter’s frequency response — which frequencies it lets through (green) and which it blocks (red).

  
    Low cutoff (high-pass):
      1.0 Hz
    
    
  
  
    High cutoff (low-pass):
      40 Hz
    
    
  
  
  
    
    
  




Experiment:

Set the low cutoff to 1 Hz and high cutoff to 40 Hz. This is the standard clinical EEG filter. The signal should look clean.
Now lower the high cutoff to 15 Hz. You’ll lose the beta rhythm. Lower it to 8 Hz — now even alpha disappears. You’re over-filtering.
Set the high cutoff back to 40 Hz and raise the low cutoff to 8 Hz. Now you’re only seeing alpha and above — the theta and delta rhythms are gone. Good for highlighting alpha, bad if you need to see sleep slow waves.
Watch the frequency response plot on the right. The green circles are components that survive the filter; the red Xs are blocked.

title: Module 3: Filtering
section: The Notch Filter: Surgical Noise Removal
text:
The Notch Filter: Surgical Noise Removal
Sometimes you don’t want to block a range of frequencies — you want to remove a single, specific frequency. The classic example: 60 Hz power line noise (50 Hz in Europe). A notch filter cuts out a narrow band around one frequency and leaves everything else untouched.
The widget below shows a signal with clear 10 Hz brain activity contaminated by 60 Hz line noise. Toggle the notch filter and watch it disappear — both in the time domain (left) and the frequency domain (right).

  
    
      Notch Filter: OFF
    
  
  
    60 Hz line noise is contaminating the signal.
  
  
    
    
  




Watch carefully:

With the notch OFF, look at the time domain signal — the fast 60 Hz oscillation rides on top of the slower alpha wave, making it look “hairy.”
Toggle the notch ON. The signal instantly smooths out, and in the power spectrum, the 60 Hz peak disappears while the 10 Hz peak stays perfectly intact.
Toggle back and forth a few times. This is exactly what happens in the clinic when you apply a notch filter.



The notch filter tradeoff: A notch filter at 60 Hz removes power line noise beautifully. But it also removes any brain activity that happens to be at 60 Hz — specifically, gamma oscillations, which are involved in attention, perception, and binding of sensory information. In most clinical settings, this is an acceptable tradeoff (gamma is hard to see on scalp EEG anyway). But in research settings where gamma activity matters, alternatives like shielded rooms and careful grounding are used instead.

title: Module 3: Filtering
section: Filter Rules of Thumb for Clinical EEG
text:
Filter Rules of Thumb for Clinical EEG
Here’s what you’ll see on most EEG machines:








Setting
Typical Value
What It Does




High-pass filter
0.5–1 Hz
Removes electrode drift and DC offset


Low-pass filter
35–70 Hz
Removes muscle artifact and high-frequency noise


Notch filter
60 Hz (US) / 50 Hz (EU)
Removes power line interference


Standard display
1–40 Hz band-pass
The “default view” — clean enough for most clinical reads



These are display filters — they change what you see, not what’s stored. Modern digital EEG systems record the raw data and let you adjust the filters afterward. This is one of the great advantages of digital over analog: you can always go back and re-filter.

title: Module 3: Filtering
section: Why Would a Doctor Care About This?
text:
Why Would a Doctor Care About This?
Because filter settings change what you see, and what you see changes your diagnosis.

Too aggressive high-pass (e.g., 5 Hz): You’ll miss slow waves — including the hallmark of absence seizures (3 Hz spike-and-wave from Module 1) if they have slow components.
Too aggressive low-pass (e.g., 15 Hz): You’ll miss fast activity — including spikes, which have high-frequency content.
No notch filter: The 60 Hz noise may obscure subtle seizure patterns.
Notch filter always on: You might miss gamma-frequency abnormalities (rare, but it happens).

The attending doesn’t just “read” an EEG — they adjust the filters, re-read, adjust again. It’s an interactive process.

title: Module 3: Filtering
text:
Quiz: Check Your Understanding

  Quiz

  
    Q1: An EEG recording shows slow undulations with a period of about 5 seconds (0.2 Hz). Which filter type removes this artifact?
    
       A. Low-pass filter
    
    
       B. High-pass filter with cutoff above 0.2 Hz
    
    
       C. Notch filter at 0.2 Hz
    
    
       D. Band-pass filter centered at 0.2 Hz
    
    
  

  

  
    Q2: You apply a band-pass filter of 1–40 Hz to an EEG recording. A 50 Hz muscle artifact is present. Does it survive the filter?
    
       A. Yes — the filter only removes frequencies below 1 Hz
    
    
       B. No — 50 Hz is above the 40 Hz high cutoff, so it's removed
    
    
       C. Partially — the filter attenuates it but doesn't remove it completely
    
    
       D. You need a notch filter for muscle artifacts
    
    
  

  

  
    Q3: A notch filter at 60 Hz removes power line noise. Could it also remove brain activity at 60 Hz?
    
       A. No — the filter can distinguish between noise and brain signals
    
    
       B. Yes — gamma oscillations near 60 Hz would be attenuated too
    
    
       C. No — brain activity doesn't occur at 60 Hz
    
    
       D. Only if the brain signal is stronger than the noise

title: Module 3: Filtering
text:
Summary
Filtering is your first line of defense against noise. The key ideas:

High-pass filters remove slow drift (low frequencies). Standard EEG: 0.5–1 Hz cutoff.
Low-pass filters remove fast noise and muscle artifact (high frequencies). Standard EEG: 35–70 Hz cutoff.
Band-pass filters combine both — they define the window of frequencies you care about.
Notch filters surgically remove a single frequency (typically 60 Hz power line noise).
Every filter is a tradeoff — removing noise always risks removing signal. Clinical judgment determines the right balance.

The signal is clean. But right now we’re looking at wiggly lines in the time domain. What if we could peek inside and see exactly which frequencies are present, and how strong each one is? That’s the Fourier Transform — and it’s the single most important tool in signal processing.

← Module 2: Sampling Module 4: FFT →

title: Module 5b: Montages & Dipoles
text:
You’re reviewing an EEG with the attending neurologist. The EEG technician asks: “Doctor, would you like the recording displayed in referential or bipolar montage?” The attending says, “Show me the banana.” You have no idea what fruit has to do with neurology. But you need to learn — because the same brain activity can look completely different depending on how you display it. And one of those displays makes a seizure source obvious. The other can hide it.
You’ve learned where the electrodes go (the 10-20 system) and what the frequency bands mean. But there’s a piece missing between “recording the signal” and “reading the EEG”: the montage. This is how EEG signals get wired up for display — and it changes everything about what you see.

title: Module 5b: Montages & Dipoles
text:
What Is a Montage?
The 10-20 system places electrodes in fixed positions on the scalp — that part doesn’t change. A montage is the math that happens after recording: how each displayed channel is built from the raw electrode voltages.
Here’s the fundamental principle: EEG measures voltage differences. A single electrode carries no meaningful voltage on its own — it needs a second point to compare against. Every channel on an EEG display is always a difference:
\[V_{\text{channel}} = V_{\text{electrode A}} - V_{\text{electrode B}}\]
The montage defines which electrode is A and which is B. Change the pairing, and the same brain activity produces a completely different-looking trace.

Analogy: Measuring elevation. You can express the height of a mountain relative to sea level — one fixed reference for everything — that’s a referential montage. Or as the slope between two neighboring points along a trail — that’s a bipolar montage. Same mountain, different description.

title: Module 5b: Montages & Dipoles
text:
The Five Montages
Clinical EEG is displayed in one of five standard montages. Each is a different linear mapping from the 19 raw electrode voltages to the channels you see on screen:








Montage
How each channel is built
What it’s good at




Longitudinal bipolar (double banana)
Electrode − neighbor along a front-to-back chain
Localizing focal activity via phase reversal — the clinical default


Transverse bipolar
Electrode − neighbor along a left-to-right chain
Catching lateralized or inter-hemispheric asymmetries


Referential (Cz)
Every electrode − Cz
Amplitude mapping across the scalp (but Cz itself is always 0)


Average reference
Every electrode − mean of all
“Unbiased” view; standard in quantitative EEG / research


Laplacian
Electrode − mean of its 4 spatial neighbors
Sharpening focal sources; suppressing diffuse activity



You’ll meet all five in the interactive at the end of this module. But first, let’s drill into the clinical default — the longitudinal bipolar, nicknamed the double banana — because it’s what every neurologist defaults to when they sit down to read a study.

title: Module 5b: Montages & Dipoles
text:
The Double Banana Montage
In a bipolar montage, each channel shows the voltage difference between two neighboring electrodes along a chain. The double banana has four chains running from front to back:








Chain
Electrodes
Derivations




Left parasagittal
Fp1 → F3 → C3 → P3 → O1
Fp1-F3, F3-C3, C3-P3, P3-O1


Right parasagittal
Fp2 → F4 → C4 → P4 → O2
Fp2-F4, F4-C4, C4-P4, P4-O2


Left temporal
Fp1 → F7 → T3 → T5 → O1
Fp1-F7, F7-T3, T3-T5, T5-O1


Right temporal
Fp2 → F8 → T4 → T6 → O2
Fp2-F8, F8-T4, T4-T6, T6-O2



The name comes from the shape of the chains — two curved lines running front-to-back on each side, like bananas. (Neurologists did not name this.)
Why is this montage the clinical default? Because EEG pathology — seizures, focal slowing, spikes — usually has a location. The front-to-back chains let you track where activity is strongest along the anterior-posterior axis. If a spike appears in F3-C3 and C3-P3 but not in Fp1-F3 or P3-O1, you know it’s centered around C3. If the left temporal chain lights up but the right doesn’t, it’s lateralized to the left.

Think about it: If each chain runs from front to back, what kind of activity would the double banana miss or underestimate? (Hint: what about activity that’s purely lateral — differences between left and right hemispheres at the same anterior-posterior level?)

title: Module 5b: Montages & Dipoles
section: The Dipole: Why EEG Looks the Way It Does
text:
The Dipole: Why EEG Looks the Way It Does
Before we can understand phase reversal properly, we need to understand what EEG actually measures. Not just “brain activity” — specifically, the electrical dipoles created by cortical pyramidal neurons.
When a group of pyramidal neurons receives synaptic input, current flows along their long apical dendrites. This creates a tiny current dipole — a separation of positive and negative charge, like a miniature battery. One end of the neuron is a current sink (negative), the other is a current source (positive).
A single neuron’s dipole is far too small to detect at the scalp. But when millions of pyramidal neurons fire in synchrony — all oriented the same way, all producing dipoles at the same time — their fields sum up. That summed dipole is what the EEG electrode detects, after the signal has been smeared and attenuated by cerebrospinal fluid, skull, and scalp.
Two important consequences:

EEG has limited spatial resolution. You’re seeing the summed activity of millions of neurons, blurred through centimeters of tissue. You can’t pinpoint activity to a few millimeters — that requires intracranial electrodes.
Dipole orientation matters. Pyramidal neurons on the crown of a gyrus produce radial dipoles that point straight toward the scalp — these are well-detected by nearby electrodes. But neurons on the wall of a sulcus produce tangential dipoles that point sideways — these produce a weaker, more spread-out signal, or may be nearly invisible to surface EEG. About two-thirds of the cortical surface is buried in sulci. A lot of brain activity never makes it to the scalp.


Why this matters clinically: Not all seizures show up on scalp EEG. A seizure originating deep in a sulcus might produce tangential dipoles that are invisible to surface electrodes. This is one reason why some epilepsy patients need intracranial EEG — electrodes placed directly on or in the brain — to find the seizure focus.

title: Module 5b: Montages & Dipoles
text:
Phase Reversal: Localizing the Source

Clinical convention — negative is up. Real clinical EEG displays voltage with negative values pointing upward. This is the opposite of a normal math plot. It’s arbitrary (a 1950s convention), but universal in neurology. The widgets below follow this convention, so “up” means “more negative voltage” — the direction flips your signal-processing intuition by 180°.

Now the payoff. In a bipolar montage, when there’s a focal electrical source (a spike, a seizure onset), the two channels that share the electrode closest to the source will deflect in opposite directions. This is a phase reversal.
Why does this happen? Think about the math. Say electrode C3 has the largest negative voltage (it’s closest to the source). In the channel F3-C3 you compute (F3 − C3): F3 is less-negative than C3, so F3 − C3 is a positive number. On an EEG display (negative-up), positive values deflect down, so F3-C3 deflects down. In the next channel, C3-P3, you compute (C3 − P3): C3 very-negative minus P3 less-negative gives a large negative number — which deflects up.
Down in one channel, up in the next. The deflection reverses at C3. That’s the phase reversal, and it points you straight at the source.

Remember Module 1? You shifted two waves by 180 degrees and watched them become perfectly out of phase — when one went up, the other went down. That’s exactly what happens at a phase reversal. Same concept, now with real clinical stakes.

Try it yourself. The widget below simulates a bipolar montage with a movable spike source. Select where the spike originates, and watch the phase reversal follow it.



  
    
      Spike source electrode:
      
        F3 (frontal)
        C3 (central)
        P3 (parietal)
        O1 (occipital)
      
    
    
      Phase reversal at C3
    
  

  

  
    The spike source is at C3. Notice: Fp1-F3 shows little deflection, F3-C3 deflects DOWN, C3-P3 deflects UP, and P3-O1 shows little deflection. The direction reversal between F3-C3 and C3-P3 is the phase reversal — it tells you C3 is the source.
  




Experiment:

Start with the source at C3 (default). See the phase reversal between F3-C3 and C3-P3? One deflects down, the other deflects up — on a clinical EEG display, “up” means more negative voltage.
Move the source to P3. The phase reversal shifts — now it’s between C3-P3 and P3-O1.
Try F3 — the phase reversal is between Fp1-F3 and F3-C3.
Now try Fp1 or O1 — what happens? The source is at the end of the chain, so there’s only one neighboring channel. You get a deflection, but no full reversal. This is a real clinical limitation.



Phase reversal summary: In a bipolar montage, find the two adjacent channels where the deflection flips direction. The electrode they share is closest to the source. This is how neurologists localize spikes and seizure onsets on an EEG — and it’s the most important pattern recognition skill for reading a bipolar montage.

title: Module 5b: Montages & Dipoles
text:
One Dipole, Five Montages — Interactive
Time to revisit all five montages now that you know what phase reversal looks like. Pick a montage, pick a spike source, and watch two things at once: the stacked channel traces (what a neurologist sees) and the scalp topographic map (the spatial voltage field, re-referenced to match the montage’s implied reference).
The underlying dipole doesn’t change. What changes is the lens — both the channel layout and the zero point the topomap is measured from.

  
    Montage:
    

    
      
        Spike source:
        
          Fp1
          F3
          F7
          C3
          T3
          P3
          T5
          O1
          Cz
          Pz
        
      
      ⏸ Pause
      
        t = 0.00 s
        
      
    
  

  
    
    
  

  
    Pick a montage and source to begin.
  




The montage is a lens, not the signal. The scalp topomap is the ground truth — the actual voltage at every point on the scalp. Every montage you can display is a linear combination of those 19 values. That’s why source localization algorithms work on the raw, un-montaged data — any single montage throws information away or reshapes it for the human eye.

title: Module 5b: Montages & Dipoles
text:
Quiz: Check Your Understanding

  Quiz

  
    Q1: In a bipolar montage, what does a "phase reversal" tell you?
    
       A. The signal has been filtered incorrectly
    
    
       B. The electrode where the phase reversal occurs is closest to the source of the activity
    
    
       C. The patient moved during the recording
    
    
       D. Two electrodes have become disconnected
    
    
  

  

  
    Q2: Why can't surface EEG detect activity from tangential dipoles in deep sulci very well?
    
       A. The skull blocks tangential electric fields completely
    
    
       B. Tangential dipoles are too small to measure
    
    
       C. The dipole is oriented parallel to the scalp surface, producing minimal voltage difference at nearby electrodes
    
    
       D. Sulcal neurons don't produce dipoles
    
    
  

  

  
    Q3: The double banana montage runs chains of electrodes from front to back. It is best at localizing activity along which axis?
    
       A. Left-right (lateral)
    
    
       B. Anterior-posterior (front to back)
    
    
       C. Superior-inferior (top to bottom)
    
    
       D. It localizes equally well in all directions

title: Module 5b: Montages & Dipoles
text:
Summary
You now understand the layer between raw electrode recordings and what appears on the screen:

A montage defines how signals are displayed, not where electrodes go. It’s the pairing of electrodes into channels.
The double banana montage is the clinical workhorse — four front-to-back chains of bipolar derivations, best at anterior-posterior localization.
Cortical dipoles are the source of EEG signals. Radial dipoles (on gyrus crowns) are well-detected; tangential dipoles (in sulci) may be invisible. This limits what surface EEG can see.
Phase reversal is the key localization tool in bipolar montages — the electrode where the deflection flips direction is closest to the source.

Next up: not everything on that EEG screen is brain activity. Eye blinks, muscle tension, heartbeats, and loose electrodes all contaminate the recording. Time to learn the difference.

title: Module 2: Sampling
text:
A technician is setting up an EEG for a patient with suspected temporal lobe epilepsy. The attending neurologist glances at the machine settings and asks: “What sampling rate are you using?” The tech replies: “256 Hz.” The attending nods and moves on. But why 256? Why not 100? Why not 10,000? And what happens if you get it wrong?
You now know what signals look like — smooth, continuous waves with amplitude and frequency. But here’s the problem: your body produces continuous signals (voltage that changes every infinitesimal instant), and computers only understand discrete numbers (a list of measurements at specific times). To get that EEG onto a screen, we need to sample it — measure the voltage at regular intervals.
How often is often enough? That’s the central question of this module. Get it right, and you faithfully capture the brain’s activity. Get it wrong, and the signal you see on screen is a lie.

title: Module 2: Sampling
text:
From Continuous to Discrete
Imagine someone singing a smooth, continuous note. Now imagine you can only listen for a brief instant once per second. If the note changes slowly, you’d follow along fine. But if the note is wobbling rapidly — faster than you’re checking — you’d miss what’s happening. Worse, you might think you’re hearing something that isn’t there.
That’s sampling. The rate at which you “check” the signal is called the sampling rate (or sampling frequency, \(f_s\)), measured in Hz — samples per second.

title: Module 2: Sampling
text:
Watch: Sampling & Aliasing in Action
Before you experiment with the interactive widgets, watch this 15-second animation. It shows a 10 Hz signal sampled at three different rates — fast enough (50 Hz), barely enough (12 Hz), and too slow (8 Hz, causing aliasing). Notice how undersampling creates a phantom 2 Hz wave that doesn’t exist in the original signal.

  
    
    Your browser does not support the video tag.
  
  
    Blue = true 10 Hz signal  |  Green = good samples  |  Red = aliased reconstruction

title: Module 2: Sampling
text:
Interactive: Sampling Visualizer
Watch what happens as you change the sampling rate. The blue curve is the “real” continuous signal. The red dots are the discrete samples your computer would capture. The dashed line connects those dots — that’s what the computer thinks the signal looks like.



  
    Signal frequency:
      5.0 Hz
    
    
  
  
    Sampling rate:
      40 Hz
    
    
  
  
  




Experiment:

Start with the signal at 5 Hz and sampling rate at 40 Hz. The dots land right on the wave — no problem.
Now slowly lower the sampling rate. Watch the dashed reconstruction start to deviate from the blue truth.
Drop the sampling rate below 10 Hz (twice the signal frequency). The reconstruction becomes completely wrong — it looks like a different signal entirely.
Try a 10 Hz signal sampled at 11 Hz. The reconstruction is a slow, ghostly wave that doesn’t exist in reality. That ghost is called an alias.

title: Module 2: Sampling
text:
The Nyquist Rule
Harry Nyquist figured this out in the 1920s: to faithfully capture a signal of frequency \(f\), you must sample at a rate of at least \(2f\) — twice the highest frequency present.
\[f_s \geq 2 \times f_{max}\]
Below that threshold, the signal aliases — it masquerades as a lower frequency. The math is surprisingly clean: a signal at frequency \(f\) sampled at rate \(f_s\) will appear at the aliased frequency \(|f - n \cdot f_s|\), where \(n\) is the nearest integer.
The widget below makes this visceral. A fixed 10 Hz signal, and you control the sampling rate.

  
    Sampling rate:
      50 Hz
    
    
  
  
  




The Nyquist moment:

Start at 50 Hz. The samples clearly trace the 10 Hz wave.
Slowly lower the sampling rate toward 20 Hz. It still works, but barely.
Drop below 20 Hz. A dashed red alias wave appears — the computer now “sees” a completely different frequency.
Try exactly 20 Hz (the Nyquist limit). Notice the samples land on zero crossings — you get zero amplitude. The Nyquist rate is the theoretical minimum; in practice, you need more than \(2f\).



The Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem: To capture a signal containing frequencies up to \(f_{max}\) Hz, you must sample at \(f_s > 2 f_{max}\). Below this rate, high-frequency content folds back as lower-frequency aliases — phantom signals that don’t actually exist in the patient’s body. Clinical systems typically sample at 4–5 times the maximum frequency of interest as a safety margin.

title: Module 2: Sampling
text:
Clinical Sampling Rates
In practice, nobody samples at exactly \(2f_{max}\) — that’s the bare minimum, and any imperfection causes aliasing. Real systems add a generous safety margin. Here’s what you’ll see in clinical settings:









Signal Type
Frequencies of Interest
Minimum Nyquist Rate
Typical Clinical Rate




EEG
0.5–100 Hz
200 Hz
256–512 Hz


ECG (Holter)
0.5–100 Hz
200 Hz
125–250 Hz


ECG (diagnostic)
0.5–150 Hz
300 Hz
500–1000 Hz


EMG
20–500 Hz
1000 Hz
1000–5000 Hz



Why 256 Hz for EEG? Because the highest frequency neurologists typically care about is around 100 Hz (the upper end of gamma activity). \(2 \times 100 = 200\) Hz minimum. 256 gives a comfortable margin and happens to be a power of 2 (which makes the computer math faster — we’ll see why in the FFT module).
The widget below lets you explore this logic for any signal type.

  Sampling Rate Calculator
  
    Select a signal type:
    
      EEG (0.5–100 Hz)
    
    
      ECG Holter (0.5–100 Hz)
    
    
      ECG Diagnostic (0.5–150 Hz)
    
    
      EMG (20–500 Hz)
    
    
      Custom
    
  
  
    Maximum frequency of interest (Hz):
    
    
      Calculate
    
  
  




Explore:

Click each signal type. Notice how much higher the practical rate is compared to the bare Nyquist minimum.
Try “Custom” — enter 500 Hz (upper EMG range). The Nyquist minimum is 1000 Hz, but you’d want 2000+ Hz in practice.
Now enter 30 Hz (just EEG alpha and beta). The minimum is only 60 Hz — but clinically we’d still use 128 or 256 Hz for safety.



Why this matters clinically: If a technician accidentally sets the EEG sampling rate too low — say 100 Hz when trying to capture high gamma activity up to 80 Hz — the system is right at the Nyquist edge. Any frequency above 50 Hz will alias down, potentially creating false patterns in the recording that look like real brain activity but aren’t. Aliasing doesn’t announce itself. The signal looks perfectly normal — just wrong. That’s what makes it dangerous.

title: Module 2: Sampling
text:
What About Anti-Aliasing?
One more thing. In real clinical systems, there’s a hardware filter before the analog-to-digital converter called an anti-aliasing filter. It’s a low-pass filter (we’ll learn about those in the next module) that removes any frequency above \(f_s/2\) before sampling. This guarantees nothing above the Nyquist frequency makes it into the digitized signal.
You don’t need to build one — the equipment manufacturer does. But you should know it exists, because it means the sampling rate you choose determines the highest frequency you can ever see in the recording. Set \(f_s\) = 256 Hz, and frequencies above 128 Hz are gone forever — the anti-aliasing filter removed them before they were recorded.

title: Module 2: Sampling
text:
Quiz: Check Your Understanding

  Quiz

  
    Q1: An EEG system samples at 256 Hz. What is the highest frequency it can faithfully capture?
    
       A. 256 Hz
    
    
       B. 128 Hz
    
    
       C. 512 Hz
    
    
       D. 64 Hz
    
    
  

  

  
    Q2: You're recording EMG, which contains frequencies up to 500 Hz. Your system samples at 800 Hz. Is this adequate?
    
       A. Yes — 800 Hz is plenty
    
    
       B. No — you need at least 1000 Hz
    
    
       C. It depends on the patient
    
    
       D. No — you need at least 500 Hz
    
    
  

  

  
    Q3: A 60 Hz signal is sampled at 50 Hz. What frequency does the aliased signal appear to be?
    
       A. 60 Hz — aliasing doesn't change the frequency
    
    
       B. 50 Hz
    
    
       C. 10 Hz
    
    
       D. 110 Hz

title: Module 2: Sampling
text:
Summary
We captured the signal digitally. The key ideas:

Sampling converts a continuous signal into discrete numbers at regular intervals.
The Nyquist theorem says: sample at more than \(2 \times f_{max}\) or you’ll get aliasing.
Aliasing creates phantom frequencies in your recording — signals that look real but aren’t.
Clinical systems use generous safety margins (4–5 times the maximum frequency of interest).
Anti-aliasing filters in the hardware prevent aliasing before it happens.

The signal is now digital. But look at it — there’s noise everywhere. Power line hum at 60 Hz, slow electrode drift, muscle artifacts. How do we clean it up without throwing away the brain activity? That’s filtering.

title: EEG & Signal Processing
text:
You’re training to become a doctor. At some point — maybe in neurology, maybe in the ICU, maybe in the OR — you will look at a screen showing electrical signals from a patient’s brain, heart, or muscles.
This tutorial will teach you to read those signals. Not just glance at them — actually understand what you’re seeing.

title: Module 1: Signals
text:
A 28-year-old woman comes to your clinic with episodes of “staring off” lasting 10–15 seconds, happening several times a day. Her partner says she doesn’t respond during these episodes. You order an EEG. The technician hands you a tracing. It looks like this:
Can you tell what’s happening? When does the abnormal activity start? What’s different about it?
By the end of this tutorial, you’ll be able to answer those questions — and understand why the signal looks the way it does. But first, we need to learn the language that describes any electrical signal from the body.

title: Module 1: Signals
text:
The Basics
Every cell in your body produces tiny electrical signals. Neurons fire. Heart muscle contracts. Skeletal muscle twitches. These are not abstract physics — these are your patient’s physiology, translated into voltage over time.
A signal is just a measurement that changes over time. When we record an EEG, ECG, or EMG, we’re measuring voltage (in microvolts or millivolts) at each moment.
The simplest signal you can imagine is a smooth, repeating wave. Three properties describe it completely: amplitude — how tall the wave is, which tells you how strong the signal is; frequency — how fast it repeats, in cycles per second; and phase — where in its cycle the wave sits at any given moment. Three knobs. Let’s play with them.

Experiment:

Set the frequency to 10 Hz. This is roughly the speed of the alpha rhythm — the dominant EEG wave when you close your eyes.
Now crank it to 1 Hz. This is the delta range — slow waves you see in deep sleep.
Set amplitude to 2 μV — that’s a weak EEG signal. Now set it to 10 μV — stronger. In real EEG, amplitudes are typically 10–100 μV.

title: Module 1: Signals
text:
Phase Across Electrodes
You’ve already played with phase in the signal builder above. Here’s why neurologists care: when two nearby electrodes record signals that are out of phase with each other, that difference is often the clue that tells you where a seizure is starting.
Drag the slider below. Both waves have the same frequency and amplitude — only the phase differs, as if two electrodes were sitting on opposite sides of the same generator.

  
    Phase offset:
      0°
    
    
  
  
  
    Both waves are identical. Try shifting the purple wave by 180° — now they're perfectly out of sync.
  




This matters clinically. Neurologists use phase reversal — where one electrode’s signal flips relative to its neighbor — to pinpoint exactly where a seizure starts. Same concept as the slider above, real clinical stakes.

title: Module 1: Signals
text:
Why Does This Matter Clinically?
Different brain states produce different frequencies and amplitudes:









Brain State
Dominant Frequency
Typical Amplitude
What’s Happening




Deep sleep
1–4 Hz (delta)
100–200 μV
Large populations of neurons firing in sync


Relaxed, eyes closed
8–13 Hz (alpha)
30–50 μV
Thalamo-cortical idle rhythm


Focused attention
13–30 Hz (beta)
10–20 μV
Cortical neurons busy with different tasks


Active thinking
>30 Hz (gamma)
5–10 μV
Local, fast processing



Notice the pattern: slower rhythms tend to be bigger, faster rhythms tend to be smaller. This isn’t a coincidence — slow rhythms reflect more neurons firing together (synchrony), which sums to a larger voltage at the scalp.

Amplitude tells you how much electrical activity is reaching the electrode, and frequency tells you how fast that activity is oscillating. Together they are the first two things you assess when you look at any physiological recording — EEG, ECG, or EMG.

title: Module 1: Signals
text:
It’s Not Just the Brain
The same two properties — amplitude and frequency — describe signals from other parts of the body:









Signal
Typical Amplitude
Typical Frequency
Source




EEG (brain)
10–100 μV
1–100 Hz
Cortical pyramidal neurons


ECG (heart)
0.5–3 mV
~1 Hz (60 bpm)
Cardiac muscle depolarization


EMG (muscle)
0.1–5 mV
20–500 Hz
Motor unit action potentials



The ECG is about 1,000× larger than the EEG. The EMG has much higher frequencies than either. These differences aren’t trivia — they determine what equipment you need, what filters you set, and what artifacts you worry about. (More on artifacts in Module 6.)

text:

title: Module 1: Signals
text:
Summary
You now know three things about any physiological signal: amplitude (how tall the wave is — μV for EEG, mV for ECG and EMG), frequency (how many cycles per second, in Hz), and phase (where in the cycle it sits at any moment). You’ve also seen that real signals aren’t single pure waves — they’re sums of many components layered together, each with its own amplitude, frequency, and phase. That last idea is what makes the Fourier transform (Module 4) so useful: it takes a messy recorded signal and pulls the components back apart.
In the next module we tackle a more basic question: how does a machine capture a continuously changing biological signal and turn it into numbers a computer can work with? Spoiler — it can go wrong in interesting ways.

← Home Module 2: Sampling →

title: Module 4: FFT
text:
A neurologist is reviewing a patient’s EEG. She glances at the screen and says: “I see a lot of theta activity.” How does she know? She’s not counting cycles by hand — the machine computed a power spectrum. The Fourier Transform is doing the work behind the scenes, every time anyone looks at a frequency analysis of any physiological signal.
Your signal is clean (thanks to filtering). But looking at a wiggly line in the time domain is like listening to an orchestra and trying to identify every instrument by ear. The Fourier Transform is like a prism — it splits the signal into its individual frequency components so you can see exactly what’s inside.
This module teaches you the single most important analysis tool in signal processing: the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT). By the end, you’ll be able to look at any signal and answer: what frequencies are in there, and how strong are they?

title: Module 4: FFT
text:
The Core Idea
Any signal — no matter how messy it looks — is just a sum of simple sine waves at different frequencies. The FFT decomposes a signal into those individual components.
Think of it this way:

Time domain: you see the signal as voltage changing over time (the wiggly line)
Frequency domain: you see which frequencies are present and how strong each one is (a bar chart of “ingredients”)

The FFT converts from the first view to the second. That’s it.

Experiment:

Start with just the 3 Hz and 10 Hz components checked. The time-domain signal looks complex, but the spectrum shows exactly two clean peaks.
Now add 60 Hz. The wiggly line gets much messier — but the spectrum immediately reveals the culprit.
Turn off everything except 10 Hz. The time domain becomes a clean sine wave, and the spectrum has a single peak. The FFT is just reading the recipe.



The FFT doesn’t create information — it reorganizes it. The same signal, viewed two different ways. The time domain shows what happens when. The frequency domain shows what frequencies are present and how strong they are. Both are equally valid descriptions of the same signal.

title: Module 4: FFT
section: Frequency Resolution: Why Recording Duration Matters
text:
Frequency Resolution: Why Recording Duration Matters
Here’s a subtlety that matters in practice. Suppose your signal contains two frequencies that are close together — say 9 Hz and 11 Hz. Can the FFT tell them apart?
It depends on how long you record. The frequency resolution of the FFT is:
\[\Delta f = \frac{1}{T}\]
where \(T\) is the recording duration in seconds. A 1-second recording can resolve frequencies 1 Hz apart. A 0.5-second recording? Only 2 Hz apart — and those two peaks will blur into one.
Drag the slider and watch the two peaks separate (or merge).

  
    Recording duration:
      1.0 s
    
    
    
      Frequency resolution: 1.00 Hz
       ($\Delta f = 1/T$)
    
  

  
    
    
  

  




Frequency resolution = \(1/T\), where \(T\) is the recording duration. Want to distinguish frequencies 1 Hz apart? You need at least 1 second of data. Want 0.5 Hz resolution? Record for 2 seconds. This is a fundamental tradeoff: better frequency resolution requires longer recordings.
This matters clinically. A 1-second EEG epoch can tell you whether activity is roughly “alpha-range” or “beta-range.” But to precisely characterize a 9 Hz vs. 11 Hz rhythm, you need several seconds of clean data.


Experiment:

Set duration to 0.5 seconds. The two peaks are invisible — just one merged bump.
Slowly increase to 1 second. Can you start to see a hint of separation?
At 2-3 seconds, the two peaks are clearly distinct.
At 5 seconds, they’re razor-sharp. More data = more precision.

title: Module 4: FFT
text:
Why This Matters Clinically
The FFT is behind every frequency analysis in clinical neurophysiology:

EEG interpretation: identifying dominant rhythms (alpha, beta, theta, delta)
Sleep staging: each sleep stage has a characteristic power spectrum
Seizure detection: abnormal rhythmic activity shows up as unexpected spectral peaks
Monitoring: brain-computer interfaces and neurofeedback systems use real-time FFT
Artifact detection: 60 Hz peaks = power line noise; broad spectral bumps = muscle artifact

Every time a monitor displays a “power spectrum” or “spectral analysis” — whether it’s in the EEG lab, the OR, or the ICU — it’s running an FFT under the hood.

title: Module 4: FFT
text:

title: Module 4: FFT
text:
Summary
You can now decompose any signal into its frequency components. The FFT takes a confusing time-domain signal and reveals its “ingredients” — which frequencies are present and how strong each one is.
Three things to remember:

The FFT is a prism — it splits a mixed signal into individual frequency components
Peaks in the spectrum = frequencies in the signal — clean peaks mean periodic activity; a sharp 60 Hz peak means noise
Resolution = \(1/T\) — longer recordings give you finer frequency detail

But so far we’ve been working with clean synthetic signals — sums of perfect sine waves. Real brain signals are messier, more interesting, and carry clinical meaning. Time to meet actual EEG.

← Module 3: Filtering Module 5: EEG Basics →

title: Module 7: Spectrograms
text:
A patient in the epilepsy monitoring unit has a seizure. The neurologist pulls up the spectrogram on the review station: a sudden burst of color sweeps across the display — low frequencies first, then evolving to faster rhythmic activity, then abruptly stopping. In one image, you can see the seizure’s entire story: onset, evolution, and termination. No other single view gives you this much information this fast.
So far you’ve used the FFT to look at which frequencies are present in a signal — averaged over the whole recording. But the brain doesn’t stay in one state. A patient falls asleep, wakes up, has a seizure, recovers. The frequencies change over time. A spectrogram shows you when each frequency appears — it’s like a movie of the brain’s frequency content.

title: Module 7: Spectrograms
text:
The Idea
An FFT tells you what frequencies are present. A spectrogram tells you what frequencies are present and when.
The recipe is straightforward:

Chop the signal into short, overlapping time windows
Compute the FFT of each window
Stack the results side by side: time on the x-axis, frequency on the y-axis, color = power

That’s it. Each vertical slice of the spectrogram is just an FFT of a short segment. String them together, and you can watch frequencies come and go.

title: Module 7: Spectrograms
section: Interactive: Building a Spectrogram from Scratch
text:
Interactive: Building a Spectrogram from Scratch
This widget walks you through the construction step by step. The signal below changes its dominant frequency over time: 5 Hz for the first 3 seconds, then 15 Hz for 4 seconds, then 8 Hz for the last 3 seconds. A standard FFT would just average all three together. The spectrogram shows them separately.



  
    Step 1: The Signal
    Step 2: Chop into Windows
    Step 3: FFT Each Window
    Step 4: Spectrogram!
  
  
  
  




Walk through all four steps in order:

Step 1 — See the raw signal with its three frequency regimes.
Step 2 — See how the signal is divided into overlapping windows.
Step 3 — See the FFT of three representative windows. Each peaks at the expected frequency.
Step 4 — See the full spectrogram. Each frequency lights up during its time period.

This is exactly what clinical EEG software does — except it does it on 20+ channels simultaneously.


A spectrogram is a stack of FFTs. Each column is the frequency content of one short time window. Together they show you how the brain’s frequency mix changes over time — sleep stages, seizure evolution, drug effects, and more.

title: Module 7: Spectrograms
text:
Interactive: Design Your Own Signal
Choose the dominant frequency for three time periods. The widget generates the signal and its spectrogram in real time — watch how the frequency content shifts across the three regimes.

        Delta (2 Hz)
        Theta (5 Hz)
        Alpha (10 Hz)
        Beta (20 Hz)

Experiment with different combinations:

Set all three periods to Alpha (10 Hz) — you’ll see a single bright horizontal band that doesn’t change. Boring brain.
Set them to Delta → Alpha → Beta — a waking-up sequence. Watch the bright band move upward.
Try Beta → Delta → Alpha — a jarring drop in frequency mid-recording. Changes this abrupt almost never show up in a real brain, but the spectrogram makes even small regime shifts obvious.

title: Module 7: Spectrograms
text:
The Window Size Tradeoff
Here’s the catch with spectrograms. There’s a fundamental tradeoff — almost like a Heisenberg uncertainty principle for time-frequency analysis:

You can’t have perfect time resolution and perfect frequency resolution at the same time.


Small window = good time resolution (you know when something changed) but poor frequency resolution (frequencies are blurry)
Large window = good frequency resolution (clean, sharp frequency bands) but poor time resolution (changes are smeared over time)

The math is simple: if your window is \(T\) seconds long, your frequency resolution is \(\Delta f = 1/T\) Hz. A 1-second window gives you 1 Hz resolution. A 0.25-second window gives you 4 Hz resolution — you can’t distinguish frequencies closer than 4 Hz apart.

The time-frequency tradeoff:

Frequency resolution = 1 / window size
Time resolution = window size
You cannot improve both simultaneously. Every spectrogram is a compromise.

In clinical EEG, a window of 0.5–2 seconds is typical. For seizure detection, you want to see fast changes (shorter window). For sleep staging, you can afford longer windows (better frequency resolution).

title: Module 7: Spectrograms
text:
Clinical Applications
Spectrograms are used daily in clinical neuroscience:








Application
What You See
Window Size




Seizure detection
Evolving frequency pattern: onset → spread → termination
0.5–1s


Sleep staging
Slow progression through frequency bands (awake→NREM→REM)
2–5s


Anesthesia monitoring
Frequency shift as drugs take effect
1–2s


Brain-computer interfaces
Motor imagery → frequency changes in mu/beta bands
0.25–0.5s


ICU monitoring
Long-term trends: detecting burst suppression or status epilepticus
1–5s



In each case, the spectrogram gives clinicians a rapid visual summary that would take much longer to extract from raw EEG traces.

title: Module 5: EEG Basics
text:
You’re a junior doctor on your neurology rotation. The EEG tech rolls in the machine and starts placing electrodes on a patient’s scalp. There are 21 of them, in very specific positions. The patient looks nervous. “Does this hurt?” she asks. “Not at all,” you say. But you’re thinking: where exactly are those electrodes going, and what will they pick up?
You now have the signal processing toolkit: you know amplitude and frequency, you can sample without aliasing, filter noise, and decompose signals into frequency components. Time to point those tools at the brain.
EEG — electroencephalography — records the electrical activity of the cerebral cortex through electrodes placed on the scalp. The signal you see is the summed postsynaptic potentials of millions of cortical pyramidal neurons, filtered through cerebrospinal fluid, skull, and skin. It’s small (microvolts), it’s noisy, and it carries clinically vital information.
This module covers the three things every doctor needs to know about EEG: where the electrodes go, what the frequency bands mean, and how to recognize basic patterns.

title: Module 5: EEG Basics
text:
Where the Signal Comes From
Before we talk about electrode placement, it’s worth pausing on what the scalp electrode is actually measuring. The brain’s cortex is full of pyramidal neurons — tall, vertically oriented cells whose apical dendrites reach toward the cortical surface. When thousands of these neurons receive excitatory input at the same time, the tiny postsynaptic voltages along their dendrites sum into a much larger electrical dipole. That dipole is what your scalp electrode picks up.
Two things matter for EEG to exist at all. First, the neurons must be aligned — pyramidal cells are, but interneurons and cerebellar cells are not, which is why those structures don’t show up well on scalp EEG. Second, they must fire synchronously — a single neuron’s PSP is tiny, and by the time it reaches the scalp it’s attenuated thousands of times over. Only summed, synchronous activity survives the trip through CSF, skull, and skin.

title: Module 5: EEG Basics
text:
The 10-20 System: Where Electrodes Go
The International 10-20 System is the standard electrode placement scheme used worldwide. The “10-20” refers to the fact that electrodes are spaced at 10% or 20% intervals along the skull from standard landmarks (nasion, inion, preauricular points).
Every electrode has a name: a letter indicating the brain region, and a number indicating the hemisphere (odd = left, even = right, z = midline).
Click on any electrode below to see what it records.



  
    
      
      
      
      
      Nose (anterior)
      
      
      
      
      
      
    

    
  

  
    Click or hover on an electrode to see what brain region it overlies and what it typically records.
  




Experiment:

Click O1 or O2 (the red dots at the back). These overlie the visual cortex — the best place to see alpha rhythm.
Click Fp1 or Fp2 (the blue dots at the front). These are closest to the eyes — they pick up eye blink artifacts.
Click T3 or T4 (gold dots on the sides). These are critical for detecting temporal lobe seizures — the most common type of epilepsy.



Naming convention: The letter indicates the region (Fp = frontopolar, F = frontal, C = central, T = temporal, P = parietal, O = occipital). Odd numbers = left hemisphere, even numbers = right hemisphere, z = midline. So C3 is left central, C4 is right central, Cz is midline central. This system is universal — it’s the same in a hospital in Tokyo, Toronto, or Tunis.

title: Module 5: EEG Basics
section: EEG Frequency Bands: The Language of Brain Rhythms
text:
EEG Frequency Bands: The Language of Brain Rhythms
EEG is never a single pure rhythm — it’s a mixture of five frequency bands, each with its own name, clinical meaning, and typical amplitude. Toggle the bands on and off below, drag the sliders, and watch a realistic EEG emerge from the sum. Expand the ⓘ Clinical significance tab under any band to read the neurologist’s version.

  
    Clinical scenarios:
    
      Awake, eyes closed
    
    
      Drowsy
    
    
      Deep sleep (N3)
    
  

  

  
     Show components
  

  




Experiment:

Start from the default mix and mute every band except δ Delta. Crank delta’s amplitude. That’s what deep sleep looks like — huge, slow waves.
Now leave only α Alpha on at 40 μV. That’s the awake, eyes-closed rhythm over the occipital cortex.
Try the Deep sleep (N3) preset, then the Awake, eyes closed preset. Same brain, different state, dramatically different signal.
Toggle Show components to see each band as a dimmed dashed trace behind the composite — the building blocks you just summed.

title: Module 5: EEG Basics
section: Alpha Blocking: The First Clinical Test
text:
Alpha Blocking: The First Clinical Test
One of the first things a neurologist checks during an EEG is alpha blocking — whether the alpha rhythm disappears when the patient opens their eyes.
Why? Because the alpha rhythm is generated by the thalamo-cortical loop when the visual cortex is “idling.” When the eyes open and visual input floods the cortex, the idle rhythm is disrupted and alpha drops out, replaced by faster beta activity. If alpha doesn’t block, something may be wrong.
Toggle between eyes-closed and eyes-open conditions below.

  
    
      Eyes Closed
    
    
      Eyes Open
    
  

  
    
    
  

  
    Eyes Closed: Strong alpha rhythm (10 Hz) dominates the occipital recording. The patient is relaxed, visual cortex is "idling."
  




Alpha blocking is one of the most reliable and clinically important EEG phenomena. When a patient closes their eyes, alpha appears over the occipital regions. When they open their eyes, it disappears. If alpha fails to block with eye opening, it raises concern for cortical dysfunction. If it’s absent entirely, consider whether the patient is anxious (beta-dominant), encephalopathic (theta/delta-dominant), or whether it’s a technical issue.


Experiment:

Toggle between Eyes Closed and Eyes Open. Watch the alpha peak (10 Hz) shrink dramatically in the spectrum.
In the time domain, notice how the eyes-closed trace has a clear ~10 Hz rhythm, while the eyes-open trace looks faster and more “noisy” — that’s beta activity taking over.

title: Module 5: EEG Basics
section: Putting It Together: What You See on an EEG Report
text:
Putting It Together: What You See on an EEG Report
When you look at a clinical EEG, the report will describe:

Background activity: Is the dominant posterior rhythm alpha? What’s its frequency and symmetry?
Reactivity: Does it block with eye opening? Does it change with alertness?
Abnormalities: Any focal slowing (theta/delta in one region)? Any epileptiform discharges (spikes, sharp waves)?
Artifacts: Eye blinks, muscle, electrode, 60 Hz noise — all the non-brain stuff (covered in Module 6)

You now have the foundation for understanding all four. You know where the electrodes go, what the frequency bands mean, and what alpha blocking looks like.

title: Module 5: EEG Basics
text:
Summary
You can now read an EEG — or at least, you know the basics:

The 10-20 System places electrodes in standardized positions. Letters = brain region, odd = left, even = right, z = midline.
Five frequency bands (delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma) each carry distinct clinical meaning. Slower = bigger amplitude, faster = smaller.
Alpha blocking (disappearance of the posterior alpha rhythm with eye opening) is a fundamental sign of normal cortical reactivity.

But there’s a catch. Not everything on that EEG screen comes from the brain. Eye blinks, muscle tension, the patient’s heartbeat, a loose electrode, someone’s cell phone — all of these can contaminate the recording. Next up: how to tell artifacts from actual brain activity, and why it matters more than you might think.

title: Module 8: The Challenge
text:
A 14-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department by her parents. Over the past two days she has had episodes of “staring and not responding” lasting 30–60 seconds, sometimes with lip-smacking. Between episodes she seems confused and drowsy. The ED attending orders a stat EEG.
Here’s a real EEG recording from the CHB-MIT Scalp EEG Database on PhysioNet — a publicly available dataset of pediatric patients with epilepsy.
The file is chb03_01.edf. It should be provided with this tutorial (in assets/eeg_data/). If you need to download it yourself:

title: Module 8: The Challenge
text:
Getting started
If you don’t have mne installed:
pip install mne numpy matplotlib
Load the file:
import mne
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

raw = mne.io.read_raw_edf("assets/eeg_data/chb03_01.edf", preload=True)
print(raw.info)
print(f"Channels: {raw.ch_names}")
print(f"Sampling rate: {raw.info['sfreq']} Hz")
print(f"Duration: {raw.n_times / raw.info['sfreq']:.1f} seconds")






Note



Heads up: One channel name appears twice in the file (T8-P8). MNE auto-renames the duplicate — don’t worry about it. The header says 3600 seconds but the actual recording is shorter (~3068 s). That’s normal for this dataset.

title: Module 8: The Challenge
text:
Your five questions
That’s it — just five. Should take 20–30 minutes.

1. Orientation
How many channels are in the recording? What’s the sampling rate? How long is the recording (in seconds)?


2. Montage
Look at the channel names. Are they individual electrodes (like Fp1, C3) or bipolar pairs (like Fp1-F7, C3-P3)? What type of montage is this?


3. Find the abnormality
Scroll through the recording. Something abnormal happens. Find it.
# Interactive scroll (works in a Jupyter/IPython environment)
raw.plot(duration=10, n_channels=20, scalings='auto')
plt.show()
What time (in seconds) does it start? When does it end?


4. Spectrogram
Pick one channel. Make a spectrogram around the abnormal event you found.
# Example: spectrogram of one channel around a time of interest
ch_name = "FP1-F7"  # pick any channel
ch_idx = raw.ch_names.index(ch_name)

# Get data around the event (adjust times to bracket what you found)
tmin, tmax = 300, 500  # seconds — adjust these
start = int(tmin * raw.info['sfreq'])
stop = int(tmax * raw.info['sfreq'])
data, times = raw[ch_idx, start:stop]
data = data.flatten() * 1e6  # convert to microvolts

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4))
ax.specgram(data, NFFT=int(raw.info['sfreq'] * 2),
            Fs=raw.info['sfreq'], noverlap=int(raw.info['sfreq']),
            cmap='viridis')
ax.set_ylim(0, 40)
ax.set_xlabel('Time (s, relative to window start)')
ax.set_ylabel('Frequency (Hz)')
ax.set_title(f'Spectrogram — {ch_name}')
plt.colorbar(ax.images[0], ax=ax, label='Power (dB)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
What do you see in the spectrogram? Describe it in 1–2 sentences.


5. What’s happening?
Based on the clinical vignette and your analysis: what is happening to this patient? Answer in 2–3 sentences.

title: Module 2: Sampling – From Continuous Body to Discrete Computer
text: This tutorial walks through the concepts step by step. Each section includes an explanation, an example, and an exercise.

title: Module 2: Sampling – From Continuous Body to Discrete Computer
text:
Step 1: Setup
Import our standard libraries for this module.


Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
%matplotlib inline

plt.rcParams['figure.figsize'] = (10, 4)
plt.rcParams['figure.dpi'] = 100
plt.rcParams['axes.grid'] = True
plt.rcParams['grid.alpha'] = 0.3
print('Ready!')


Exercise 1: Try it yourself!

title: Module 2: Sampling – From Continuous Body to Discrete Computer
text:
Step 2: From Continuous to Discrete
Clinical scenario: A technician is setting up an EEG for a patient with suspected temporal lobe epilepsy. The attending neurologist asks: “What sampling rate are you using?” The tech replies: “256 Hz.” Why 256? Why not 100? Why not 10,000? And what happens if you get it wrong?
Your body produces continuous signals – voltage that changes every infinitesimal instant. Computers only understand discrete numbers – a list of measurements at specific times.
Sampling means measuring the voltage at regular intervals. The rate at which you “check” the signal is called the sampling rate (\(f_s\)), measured in Hz (samples per second).
Exercise 2: Try it yourself!

title: Module 2: Sampling – From Continuous Body to Discrete Computer
text:
Step 3: Visualizing Sampling
Let’s see what happens when we sample a continuous signal at different rates. The continuous signal (blue) is the “truth.” The red dots are the discrete samples the computer captures.


Code
def plot_sampling(signal_freq, sampling_rate, duration=1.0):
    """Show continuous signal with discrete samples overlaid."""
    # 'Continuous' signal (high resolution)
    t_cont = np.arange(0, duration, 1/2000)
    y_cont = np.sin(2 * np.pi * signal_freq * t_cont)
    
    # Sampled points
    t_samp = np.arange(0, duration, 1/sampling_rate)
    y_samp = np.sin(2 * np.pi * signal_freq * t_samp)
    
    # Determine status
    aliased = sampling_rate < 2 * signal_freq
    status = 'ALIASED!' if aliased else 'OK'
    status_color = '#c0392b' if aliased else '#27ae60'
    
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 3.5))
    plt.plot(t_cont, y_cont, color='#2980b9', linewidth=2, label='Continuous signal')
    plt.plot(t_samp, y_samp, 'o', color='#e74c3c', markersize=6, label=f'Samples (fs={sampling_rate} Hz)')
    # Connect samples with dashed line (reconstruction)
    plt.plot(t_samp, y_samp, '--', color='#e74c3c', linewidth=1, alpha=0.5, label='Reconstruction')
    
    plt.title(f'{signal_freq} Hz signal sampled at {sampling_rate} Hz -- {status}',
              color=status_color, fontweight='bold')
    plt.xlabel('Time (seconds)')
    plt.ylabel('Amplitude')
    plt.legend()
    plt.ylim(-1.5, 1.5)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

# Good sampling: 5 Hz signal at 40 Hz
plot_sampling(signal_freq=5, sampling_rate=40)

# Marginal: 5 Hz signal at 12 Hz
plot_sampling(signal_freq=5, sampling_rate=12)

# Undersampled: 5 Hz signal at 8 Hz
plot_sampling(signal_freq=5, sampling_rate=8)


Exercise 3: Try it yourself!

title: Module 2: Sampling – From Continuous Body to Discrete Computer
section: Step 4: Nyquist Demo: Aliasing a 10 Hz Signal
text:
Step 4: Nyquist Demo: Aliasing a 10 Hz Signal
The Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem: To faithfully capture a signal of frequency \(f\), you must sample at at least \(2f\) Hz.
\[f_s \geq 2 \times f_{max}\]
Below that threshold, the signal aliases – it masquerades as a lower frequency. The aliased frequency is: \(f_{alias} = |f_{signal} - n \cdot f_s|\) where \(n\) is the nearest integer.
Let’s sample a fixed 10 Hz signal at three different rates and see what happens.


Code
fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(10, 8), sharex=True)

f_signal = 10  # Hz
t_cont = np.arange(0, 1.0, 1/2000)  # 'Continuous'
y_cont = np.sin(2 * np.pi * f_signal * t_cont)

rates = [
    (50, '#27ae60', 'fs=50 Hz (good) -- well above 2x10=20 Hz'),
    (12, '#e67e22', 'fs=12 Hz (marginal) -- barely above Nyquist'),
    (8, '#c0392b', 'fs=8 Hz (ALIASED) -- below 2x10=20 Hz'),
]

for ax, (fs, color, title) in zip(axes, rates):
    t_samp = np.arange(0, 1.0, 1/fs)
    y_samp = np.sin(2 * np.pi * f_signal * t_samp)
    
    ax.plot(t_cont, y_cont, color='#2980b9', linewidth=2, alpha=0.5, label='True 10 Hz')
    ax.plot(t_samp, y_samp, 'o-', color=color, markersize=6, linewidth=1.5, label=f'Sampled')
    
    # Show alias if undersampled
    if fs < 2 * f_signal:
        f_alias = abs(f_signal - round(f_signal / fs) * fs)
        if f_alias > 0:
            y_alias = np.sin(2 * np.pi * f_alias * t_cont)
            ax.plot(t_cont, y_alias, '--', color=color, linewidth=2,
                    label=f'Alias: {f_alias:.1f} Hz (phantom!)')
    
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=11, color=color)
    ax.legend(fontsize=9)
    ax.set_ylim(-1.5, 1.5)

axes[-1].set_xlabel('Time (seconds)')
plt.suptitle('Sampling a 10 Hz Signal at Different Rates', fontsize=13, y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()


Exercise 4: Try it yourself!

title: Module 2: Sampling – From Continuous Body to Discrete Computer
text:
Step 5: Alias Frequency Calculation
The alias frequency can be computed mathematically. When you undersample:
\[f_{alias} = |f_{signal} - n \cdot f_s|\]
where \(n = \text{round}(f_{signal} / f_s)\).
This is dangerous because the alias looks perfectly real. A 60 Hz power line signal sampled at 50 Hz would appear as 10 Hz – which could be mistaken for alpha rhythm in an EEG!


Code
def compute_alias(f_signal, f_sample):
    """Compute the aliased frequency."""
    n = round(f_signal / f_sample)
    f_alias = abs(f_signal - n * f_sample)
    return f_alias

# Examples
examples = [
    (60, 50, '60 Hz noise sampled at 50 Hz'),
    (10, 8, '10 Hz signal sampled at 8 Hz'),
    (100, 75, '100 Hz signal sampled at 75 Hz'),
    (30, 25, '30 Hz signal sampled at 25 Hz'),
]

print(f'{"Signal":>10s}  {"Sample Rate":>12s}  {"Nyquist OK?":>12s}  {"Alias Freq":>12s}')
print('-' * 52)
for f_sig, f_samp, desc in examples:
    f_alias = compute_alias(f_sig, f_samp)
    ok = 'Yes' if f_samp >= 2 * f_sig else 'NO'
    print(f'{f_sig:>8d} Hz  {f_samp:>10d} Hz  {ok:>12s}  {f_alias:>10.1f} Hz')
    print(f'  --> {desc}')

title: Module 2: Sampling – From Continuous Body to Discrete Computer
text:
Step 6: Clinical Sampling Rates
Nobody samples at exactly \(2f_{max}\) – that’s the bare minimum. Real systems add a generous safety margin:









Signal
Frequencies of Interest
Min Nyquist
Typical Clinical Rate




EEG
0.5-100 Hz
200 Hz
256-512 Hz


ECG (Holter)
0.5-150 Hz
300 Hz
128-500 Hz


EMG
20-500 Hz
1000 Hz
1000-5000 Hz



Why 256 Hz for EEG? Because the highest frequency neurologists typically care about is ~100 Hz. \(2 \times 100 = 200\) Hz minimum. 256 gives a comfortable margin and happens to be a power of 2 (which makes FFT computation faster).


Code
def sampling_calculator(signal_name, f_max):
    """Calculate recommended sampling rates."""
    nyquist = 2 * f_max
    practical = 4 * f_max
    # Nearest power of 2
    power_of_2 = 2 ** int(np.ceil(np.log2(practical)))
    
    print(f'\n{signal_name}')
    print(f'  Max frequency of interest: {f_max} Hz')
    print(f'  Minimum Nyquist rate: 2 x {f_max} = {nyquist} Hz')
    print(f'  Recommended (4x): {practical} Hz')
    print(f'  Nearest power-of-2: {power_of_2} Hz')

sampling_calculator('EEG', 100)
sampling_calculator('ECG (Holter)', 150)
sampling_calculator('EMG', 500)
